questions.question: A diverging lens is:
options: Thicker at the center than at the edges
Thinner at the center than at the edges
Uniform thickness throughout
Flat on both sides
questions.explanation: A diverging (concave) lens curves inward and is thinner in the middle, causing parallel rays to spread out.

questions.question: When incoming parallel light rays pass through a diverging lens, they:
options: Converge directly toward a real focal point behind the lens structure.
Diverge outwards as if originating from a virtual focus behind the lens.
Diverge outwards as if originating from a virtual focus before the lens.
Reflect perfectly backward toward the initial source location before the lens.
questions.explanation: A concave diverging lens causes incoming parallel light rays to spread apart upon passing through the glass. When an observer traces these separating rays backward, their virtual line extensions intersect at a virtual focal point located on the same front side of the lens where the light originally entered.

questions.question: The focal point of a diverging lens is:
options: Real and on the opposite side of the lens from the object
Virtual and on the same side of the lens as the object
Real and on the same side of the lens as the object
At infinity
questions.explanation: The focal point of a diverging lens is virtual — it lies on the same side as the incoming light, where refracted rays appear to come from.

questions.question: A diverging lens always produces an image that is:
options: Real, inverted, and diminished
Virtual, upright, and diminished
Real, upright, and magnified
Virtual, inverted, and magnified
questions.explanation: Regardless of object position, a diverging lens always forms a virtual, upright, and diminished image.

questions.question: Compared to the object, the image formed by a diverging lens is always:
options: Magnified
Same size
Diminished
Variable depending on object distance
questions.explanation: The image is always smaller than the object. Magnification is always less than 1 for a diverging lens.

questions.question: In a diverging lens, no matter where an object is placed, the image is always located:
options: Beyond the position $2F$ on the opposite side from the object.
Between the lens and $F$ on the identical side as the object.
Between the positions $F$ and $2F$ on the opposite side from the object.
Inside the position $F$ on the opposite side from the object.
questions.explanation: Because a diverging (concave) lens causes light rays to spread apart upon refraction, the refracted rays will never intersect to form a real image. Instead, when traced backward, these virtual line extensions will always intersect within the confined boundary between the center of the lens and the virtual focal point ($F$) on the same side of the lens where the object is located.

questions.question: A diverging lens is used to correct myopia (nearsightedness) because it:
options: Converges light rays before they enter the eye
Diverges light rays before they enter the eye
Magnifies distant objects
Increases the eye's focal length
questions.explanation: In myopia, the eye focuses light in front of the retina. A concave lens spreads the rays slightly, shifting the image onto the retina.

questions.question: A peephole in a door uses a diverging lens because it:
options: Magnifies the person outside for identification
Provides a wide-angle view of the outside
Focuses light to create a real image
Inverts the image for security purposes
questions.explanation: A diverging lens captures a wide field of view, allowing the viewer to see a large area outside the door.

questions.question: Unlike a convex lens, a diverging lens:
options: Can produce a real image
Can magnify objects
Always produces a virtual image
Has a real focal point
questions.explanation: A diverging lens never forms a real image. Its images are always virtual, upright, and diminished.

questions.question: When an object is located at infinity from a diverging lens, the image forms at:
options: The real focal point on the opposite side from the object.
The virtual focal point on the identical side as the object.
The double focal length on the opposite side from the object.
The central optical center on the identical side as the object.
questions.explanation: Because the object is at infinity, its incoming light rays enter the diverging lens parallel to the principal axis. The lens causes these parallel rays to spread apart upon refraction. When traced backward, these refracting rays appear to originate from a single intersection node at the virtual focal point ($F$) located on the same side of the lens as the object.

questions.question: A ray passing through the optical center of a diverging lens:
options: Bends toward the principal axis
Bends away from the principal axis
Passes straight through without bending
Is completely absorbed
questions.explanation: The optical center is the point where rays pass through undeviated — true for all thin lenses.

questions.question: The focal length of a diverging lens is considered:
options: Positive
Negative
Zero
Infinite
questions.explanation: By sign convention, the focal length of a diverging lens is negative because the focal point is virtual.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered diverging lenses and their applications!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the consistent image properties of diverging lenses.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on why the focal point is virtual and why images are always diminished.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: concave lens shape, virtual focal point, and the rule that images are always virtual, upright, and diminished.
